messages: Hi — I'm starting to learn Spanish. My goal is to be conversational for travel and basic work meetings within about six months. I studied a bit in high school but I'm pretty rusty; where should I begin?
I can realistically commit about 45 minutes a day, six days a week. Is that a sensible amount to reach conversational level in that timeframe?
If 45 minutes works, how would you divide a session between grammar, vocabulary, listening, speaking, and review? Rough percentages or a sample block schedule would help.
Which grammar points should I prioritize in the first three months? I'm guessing present tense, past tenses, articles, pronouns, and basic object pronouns — am I missing anything essential?
How many new vocabulary items should I aim to learn per week to make steady progress, and is it better to learn by topic (travel, food, work) or by frequency?
I've heard a lot about Anki and spaced repetition. Should I use premade decks or build my own? How much daily SRS time makes sense within my 45 minutes?
Would paying for a tutor or a subscription app early on be worth it to speed up speaking ability, or can I achieve similar results with free tools?
How soon should I start speaking out loud and looking for conversation partners? Should I wait until I have a basic grammar foundation or start practicing immediately?
Do you recommend shadowing for pronunciation improvement? If so, what's a simple 10-minute shadowing routine I could do daily?
For listening practice at the beginner level, what types of content should I use — graded audio, slowed speech podcasts, children's programs, or simple YouTube channels?
What reading materials are most useful early on — graded readers, bilingual books, or adapted news articles? Any starter titles you recommend?
Can you suggest realistic benchmarks for months 1, 3, and 6 so I have concrete goals (for example, vocabulary size, situations I should be able to handle)?
When practicing with native speakers or a tutor, what's the best approach to corrections so I improve without getting discouraged?
Could you sketch a sample weekly schedule that fits my 45-minute daily limit, showing which days to prioritize speaking, grammar, and review?
What micro-tasks can I do on very busy days that still move me forward — two or three things under ten minutes each?
How should I balance active study versus passive exposure like music, radio, or background TV? Any recommended ratios for a beginner?
What common pitfalls do self-learners face in the first six months, and how can I avoid them?
Could you outline a six-month syllabus with monthly themes and skills so I can track long-term progress?
If I'm willing to spend a bit of money, where would you recommend finding tutors or conversation partners, and how many paid sessions per week are optimal?
Can you provide short roleplay scripts for practical situations: ordering food, asking for directions, checking into a hotel, and small talk at a meeting?
What ratio of focused speaking practice (with correction) to casual conversation do you recommend per week for steady improvement?
Which grammar reference books or websites do you find clearest and most learner-friendly for regular consultation?
Is it better to use native content early (with transcripts) or to stick to graded learner materials until I'm more comfortable?

To be explicit about resources: I'm comfortable using internet materials and had been planning a modest budget — roughly fifty dollars a month — for a tutor or subscriptions if you think that's worth it.
Update: my employer just informed me they will no longer cover language expenses, so I have to assume I can't pay for tutors or subscriptions from here on out.
Given that change, what free alternatives for conversation practice give the most reliable speaking feedback and structure?
Which language-exchange platforms or communities are free but serious, not just casual chat? Any tips for finding committed partners there?
For structured grammar study without paying, what free websites, open textbooks, or YouTube channels are concise and reliable?
For spaced repetition, are public Anki decks usually good enough for beginners, or should I invest time creating tailored cards even with no budget?
Where can I find high-quality, beginner-friendly listening material for free—public radio, certain podcasts, YouTube series, or other sources you trust?
If I can't pay for corrections, how can I still get accurate feedback on pronunciation and speaking mistakes at no cost?
I have access to a local library and community resources—what exactly should I look for there (graded readers, audio kits, conversation groups) to stretch free options?
Are university language tandems, volunteer tutors, or community meetups effective alternatives, and how should I approach them to get regular practice?
Given these free options, should I still target the same six-month conversational goal with my 45-minute schedule, or would you recommend adjusting expectations?
Okay — assuming paid services aren't an option now, can you design a detailed six-month study plan that uses only free, widely available resources and still fits my 45-minute daily routine?

Please break month one into week-by-week 45-minute session plans with explicit activities (for example, which Anki deck to use, which podcast episode or YouTube lesson to listen to, and which short roleplay to practice).
Also include simple, zero-cost methods to get corrective feedback on speaking and writing that I can start using in week two and continue regularly.
Give me measurable milestones for the end of month 1, month 3, and month 6 that I can self-assess without paying for formal tests.
Provide a prioritized shortlist of the best free resources (about 8–10 entries) categorized by skill—speaking, listening, reading, grammar, and SRS—so I know where to start.
If you can format the plan as a copy-paste weekly checklist with one-line daily tasks I can tick off, that would help me stay consistent—can you prepare it that way?
